responseSet.title: The Scope of the Psychoanalyst as a Consultant in Colleges
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Scope of the Psychoanalyst as a Consultant in Colleges

Lore Rubin M.D.

Lore Rubin

Lore Reich Rubin, M.D.
4715 Wallingford Street
Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania 15213

Lore Rubin  

The panel combined an unusual number of relevant fields. Gerard Fountain, the chairman, was formerly dean of Sarah Lawrence and a college psychiatrist. William Peltz, professor of clinical psychiatry at the University of Pennsylvania, has been a member of GAP on College Students, a psychiatric consultant to two boys boarding schools, and on the discipline committee at Penn. Lore Rubin is a psychoanalyst and a consultant to the University of Pittsburgh Student Health Service. Neil Smelser, a professor of sociology and former assistant chancellor for educational development at Berkeley, is also a research training candidate at the San Francisco psychoanalytic institute. He has experience in student therapy and has served as a consultant on student political activities. Benson R. Snyder was psychiatrist-in-chief at MIT for ten years and is now dean of institute relations. He has worked with GAP, was a consultant at Wellesley and a trustee at Antioch. In addition, Fountain hoped for participation by the audience, which contained many people with student and college experience.

Fountain suggested two areas for discussion: the potential contribution of the psychoanalyst, whether on or off the campus, to the student, individually, and to the functioning of the university as the environment of the student; and possible psychoanalytic contributions to an understanding of the specific problems encountered on the campus these days, particularly student dissent.

Smelser, the only member of the panel asked to present a brief prepared discussion, began with an emphasis upon the many separate functions of the university—generation of knowledge, public service through training, allocation of labor to the skilled labor force, socialization, entertainment. Hence the complexity of the problems that arise when we relate psychoanalysis to the university environment. He dealt with four areas:

1. Relation to student health services and student therapy.

responseSet.title: The Ideological Wellsprings of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Ideological Wellsprings of Psychoanalysis

Lucille Ritvo

Lucille Ritvo

Lucille Ritvo  

Because several people including panelists had queried the term "ideological" in the title, Jacob A. Arlow explained its usage here in the dictionary sense of the "science of ideas": the panel intended to explore the world of ideas as transformed by the genius of Freud. In the absence of David Bakan, Arlow said he would try to fill in aspects of the Jewish tradition. He established the spirit of the Panel with Bakan's quotations from Freud on genius ("We know that genius is incomprehensible and unaccountable and that it should therefore not be called upon as an explanation until every other solution has failed"; "Everything new must have its roots in what was before"; "Few tasks are as appealing as inquiring into the laws that govern the psyche of exceptionally endowed individuals") and with Jones's statement that "Freud was the child of his time and we have no reason to think that he ever indulged in individual speculations beyond those prevailing in his milieu." It seemed more appropriate for a historical panel on psychoanalysis to use the "genetic approach" rather than the arbitrary alphabetical listing in the program. It therefore began with Richard Sterba's presentation of "The Humanistic Wellsping of Psychoanalysis."

Using a quotation from Peter Blos's Nunberg lecture (1969) that "Psychoanalysis has always held firmly and passionately to the humanist tradition. Nothing remains more valued and worthy of our endeavor than the harmonizing influence we can exert through our science on the life of man," Sterba pointed out that the ideology of humanistic culture which developed during the Renaissance dominated Freud's education at the Gymnasium from the ages of ten to eighteen, a period of life when the foundation of a Weltanschauung and value system is in the process of developing and becoming entrenched. The educated individual had to have at his fingertips such classical quotations as the hexameter from Virgil's

Held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Miami Beach, May, 1969. Chairman: Jacob A. Arlow, M.D.

responseSet.title: The Fate of the Defenses in the Psychoanalytic Process
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fate of the Defenses in the Psychoanalytic Process

Justin Krent M.D.

Justin Krent

Justin Krent  

Although recent panels have been concerned with the general topic of ego defenses, relatively little attention has been given to the changes in defenses which are effected by the analytic process. Justin Krent reviewed previous panels relating to these changes and summarized current concepts of defense mechanisms presented at these meetings. Selma Fraiberg traced the changes in a child's defenses as a result of analysis and provided subsequent nonanalytic observations of the same individual as a young adult. Aaron Karush, using material from the second analysis of an adult, contrasted the outcomes of defenses as a result of a difference in analytic approach. Rudolph M. Loewenstein's paper concerned theoretical considerations and, in his absence, was read by the Chairman.

Surveying previous panels, Krent reported that a difference of opinion existed among the members of panels on defense mechanisms and psychoanalytic technique held in London and New York in 1953.1 One panelist maintained that a successful analysis should result in the disappearance of defenses; another asserted that analysis resulted in the modification, not in the disappearance, of defenses and that while the neurotic individual is restricted to the use of primarily one defense mechanism in all situations, the analyzed person is free to use a variety of defenses in an adaptive manner. This difference was not settled, although the latter position seemed to be the one most favored.

The same motif appeared again in more colorful form in one of two 1966 panels on defenses.2

Held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Miami Beach, May 2, 1969. Chairman: Charles Brenner, M.D.

1See The Annual Survey of Psychoanalysis, 5:80-81, 316-327, 1954; and This Journal, 2:318-326, 1954.2See This Journal, 15:130-165, 1967.177

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Evaluation of Addiction and Habituation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Evaluation of Addiction and Habituation

William Frosch M.D.

William Frosch

William Frosch  

The World Health Organization has defined addiction as a periodic or chronic intoxication produced by the repeated consumption of a drug with a tendency to increase the dose and the development of physical dependence and an abstinence syndrome. Habituation is defined as a condition resulting from the repeated consumption of a drug because of overpowering desire, the development of psychic dependence, with detrimental effects to the individual. Because of difficulty distinguishing between these two states, the World Health Organization has more recently suggested substitution of the term drug dependency for both addiction and habituation. The term drug dependency is to be qualified by the drug involved. In his opening remarks, Eli Marcovitz suggested that psychoanalytic psychology could make a contribution to the understanding of the multiple psychologic, pharmacologic, and social factors to be considered in the understanding of drug dependency and that observation of drug-induced states could in turn contribute to psychoanalytic theory.

Early psychoanalytic formulations were that fixation occurred at early libidinal stages of development. Freud first described masturbation as a primary addiction. He later attributed smoking and drinking to oral erotism. Narcissistic and sadomasochistic components were then recognized. This was followed by an appreciation of the role of anxiety, guilt, repression, and the defenses against these, including regression, introjection, and projection. Although there are significant differences, common psychological factors permit us to describe a spectrum of dependency: thumb sucking; nail biting; eating of skin, hair or mucus; attachment to a transitional object; compulsive masturbation; extreme forms of dependence upon one or a series of love objects; compulsive eating; compulsive smoking; alcoholism and the various abuses of stimulants, hallucinogens, and narcotics.

responseSet.title: Object-Specific Superego Responses
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Object-Specific Superego Responses

Burton Wixen M.D.

Burton Wixen

Burton N. Wixen, M.D.
11860 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, Calif. 90025

Burton Wixen  

I

THIS PAPER'S PURPOSE is to draw attention to certain phenomena which, from a particular point of view, may be grouped together as object-specific superego responses. It is such a point of view which enables us to draw certain disparate phenomena together, thereby lending theoretical unity to them. There are also certain theoretical implications which will be pointed out later. Object-specific superego responses may be defined as responses in an individual which appear to be motivated by the superego (i.e. defined by superego referents), which occur only in the presence of particular individuals, or groups of individuals.

II

The paradigm for this phenomenon may be seen in the transference neurosis. Here an individual's response to the analyst may be one of great concern about what the analyst thinks about particular behavior. In a well-developed transference neurosis the person may be relatively free of anxiety or guilt in his ordinary daily relationships, but acutely anxious or guilty about particular behavior as he reports it during his analytic hour. We ordinarily refer to this as "projection of the superego."

Presented to the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Miami, Florida, May 3, 1969.

Submitted August 5, 1969

831

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="APA" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="APA.018.0539A" ISSN="0003-0651"><artyear>1970</artyear><artvol actual="18">18</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">539-561</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">On the Structural View of Affect</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Smith, Joseph H." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Joseph</nfirst><nmid>H.</nmid><nlast>Smith</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Joseph H. Smith, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>4705 Locust Hill Court</ln><ln>Bethesda, Md.</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: On the Structural View of Affect
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Structural View of Affect

Joseph Smith M.D.

Joseph Smith

Joseph H. Smith, M.D.
4705 Locust Hill Court
Bethesda, Md.

Joseph Smith  

SUMMARY

The fallacy of hypostatization of affect has occurred in two forms—originally by equating affect and energy, and recently by equating affect and structure. Both forms of this fallacy seem to foster the conceptualization of affect itself being capable of unconscious existence. In the above I have advocated retention of the prior concept of affect as conscious ego response. Such

559

a conceptualization seems necessary to the development of a general theory of affect and a general theory of signal affect.

Signal affect is best understood not simply as affect "tamed" or in small doses. It is not affect which has been changed in form or diminished in quantity, concepts which derive from the hypostatization fallacy. Nor is signal affect limited to an anxious response to an anticipated situation of danger. Signal affect in general is the correlate of highly differentiated and hierarchically integrated active ego functioning. Thus affect signals as modulated ego responses occur on the basis of anticipation, recall or in a present situation. However, it is not affect as a quantitative factor which is modulated. Rather, signal affective responses occur in situations (and ego states) which permit simultaneous attention to multiple stimuli, none of them compelling immediate or total response.

Finally, the general theory of affect would not limit the affective response of active ego functioning to signal affect. Extremes of affective response occur not only passively as in affect storms or overwhelming trauma, but also as active affective response in the actuality of singularly compelling events.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Harold M. Voth, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>Department of Research The Menninger Foundation</ln><ln>Box 829</ln><ln>Topeka, Kansas 66601</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Analysis of Metaphor
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analysis of Metaphor

Harold Voth M.D.

Harold Voth

Harold M. Voth, M.D.
Department of Research The Menninger Foundation
Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Harold Voth  

SUMMARY

Case material has been presented which illustrates that certain words and phrases may suddenly appear, interspersed with the patient's more typical language style, during phases of intense treatment. These communications are particularly susceptible to analysis because resistance is low at the moment they are spoken. The results of such focused analysis, if properly timed, lead directly to the deepest roots of the personality and provide an excellent opportunity for linking these roots to the transference, to the patient's personality, to his life experience, and to the working-through process.

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="APA" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="APA.018.0440A" ISSN="0003-0651"><artyear>1970</artyear><artvol actual="18">18</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">440-461</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Preventive Psychiatry and the Field Theory of Reality</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Gould, Roger L." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Roger</nfirst><nmid>L.</nmid><nlast>Gould</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln>Roger L. Gould, M.D.</ln><ln>ULCA Medical Center Dept. of Psychiatry</ln><ln>760 Westwood Plaza</ln><ln>Los Angeles, Calif. 90024</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Preventive Psychiatry and the Field Theory of Reality
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preventive Psychiatry and the Field Theory of Reality

Roger Gould M.D.

Roger Gould

Roger L. Gould, M.D.
ULCA Medical Center Dept. of Psychiatry
760 Westwood Plaza
Los Angeles, Calif. 90024

Roger Gould  

SUMMARY

We see community psychiatry as a public health venture that demands affecting individual internal systems by external social

459

systems. We see the social reality as a force affecting the individual's internal equilibrium via a complex of channels regulating drive discharge by complementing ego structures. We see the reality operating as a series of signs (values, expectations) mediating change demands or maintenance demands of structures. We see the signs of social reality being responded to as if emanating from a love object replacing the parents and from that source gaining relevance. We see the sticking to the adaptational tasks imposed by the social reality as being a reality guidance process propelled from within, and the symptoms generated by the adaptational processes as being the noise, which, if we follow, will lead us to the internal processes involved in the phase adaptation. We see the adaptational noise level as being affected by the constellation of the immediate actual social reality; that is, the ratio of structure-changing to structure-maintaining forces. Finally, we see the concepts of structure-maintaining and structure-changing forces as equally useful in more fully understanding the individual as well as a society of individuals.

responseSet.documentRef: Sarlin, C. N. (1970) The Current Status of the Concept of Genital Primacy. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:285-299

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="APA" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="APA.018.0285A" ISSN="0003-0651"><artyear>1970</artyear><artvol actual="18">18</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">285-299</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">The Current Status of the Concept of Genital Primacy</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Sarlin, Charles N." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Charles</nfirst><nmid>N.</nmid><nlast>Sarlin</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Charles N. Sarlin, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>Pinetop, Arizone 85935</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Current Status of the Concept of Genital Primacy
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Current Status of the Concept of Genital Primacy

Charles Sarlin M.D.

Charles Sarlin

Charles N. Sarlin, M.D.
Pinetop, Arizone 85935

Charles Sarlin  

SUMMARY

The concept of genital primacy, the end stage of psychosexual development, is based upon the capacity of the ego (and superego)

296

for the sublimation and control of both libidinal and aggressive instinctual drives.

The level of psychosexual development of the mother in every culture determines the characterological patterns of both men and women of the next generation.

There are two fundamental psychological crises in the development of all human beings, primitive or civilized. The first is the establishment of an individual identity based upon psychological separation and manifested by autonomy and independence, and leading to a realistic sense of identity, of both self and of object. It is only after separation conflicts are relatively well resolved that the next major crisis can become of paramount importance, the establishment of a sexual identity, of the characterology of masculinity or femininity consistent with anatomical reality.

There is a profound difference in the influence upon psychosexual development between archaic identifications, which arise out of a primitive defensive denial of aggression, and those which arise out of genuine libidinal drives.

The hostile aggressive aspect of narcissistic indifference to the object has been insufficiently emphasized.

Finally, the function of the evolution of permanent, nonprocreative sexuality of the human female in the neutralization of innate aggressive drives toward the achievement of genital primacy has been considered.

responseSet.title: Book Notices
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE WRITINGS OF ANNA FREUD, VOLUME IV: INDICATIONS FOR CHILD ANALYSIS AND OTHER PAPERS (1945-1956). New York: International Universities Press, 1968, xv + 690 pp., $12.00.

Volume IV is the first to appear of Anna Freud's Writings. There will be a total of seven volumes, arranged chronologically and according to certain periods of work. The papers in this particular volume are written in accordance with the nature of the audience addressed, analysts and nonanalysts, the latter including professionals as well as laymen. There are three parte, comprising a total of thirty-five papers. Among them such well-known contributions appear as "Indications for Child Analysis," "The Contributions of Psychoanalysis to Genetic Psychology," "The Role of Bodily Illness in the Mental Life of Children." A number of papers are published for the first time, while others appear which had not previously been translated.

PSYCHOSOMATIC SPECIFICITY, VOLUME I: EXPERIMENT, STUDY, AND RESULTS. Edited by Franz Alexander, Thomas M. French, and George H. Pollock. Chicago & London: University of Chicago Press, 1968, 263 pp., $7.50.

This interdisciplinary research project is built on a "Nuclear operating principle [which] involved testing our ability to arrive at a somatic diagnosis from a formulation based on the psychogenetic and psychodynamic construction of the personality structure of the patient and of the relationship of the onset and exacerbating periods of the disease to this personality structure." Seven diseases were studied by the editors and seven collaborators. These were bronchial asthma, rheumatoid arthritis, ulcerative colitis, essential hypertension, neurodermatitis, thyrotoxicosis, and duodenal peptic ulcer. At the beginning each of the personality profiles associated with these conditions is summarized, but it is also stated that these were only approximate formulations and that they have been improved and revised. Such corrected formulations, together with theoretical discussions of a more comprehensive nature, will appear in a forthcoming second volume.

[[RunningHead]]

Functional Phenomenon: Historical Concept, Contemporary Defense

Austin Silber M.D.

Austin Silber

Austin Silber, M.D.
245 East 87th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Austin Silber  

SUMMARY

"Functional phenomenon" as a historical concept is reviewed. Its appearance as a clinical event during an analytic session is described. This permitted an extension of its meaning as a concept.

The literature relating to patients falling "asleep" during analytic sessions, and related altered states of consciousness which supervene during analytic treatment are summarized.

The clinical material described encompasses a variety of defensive functions of the ego. The most dramatic feature is the

536

altered state of consciousness, the hypnoid state, caught in the act of failing as a defense and leading into another altered state dramatized by what the patient described as his "dream." This "dream image" also failed as a temporary compromise structure instituted by his ego and was followed by the patient "acting out" the "functional" aspect of the "dream" image by rising from the couch and sitting in a chair, viewing me. This sitting opposite me and viewing me directly, enhanced his ability to evaluate reality more adequately and warded off any further regression. Thus it was possible to study, in comparative isolation and from a novel historical perspective, certain rarely observed defenses of the ego, in the process of functioning, failing and ultimately adaptively stabilizing.

The theoretical significance of the specific clinical events reported upon are discussed.

responseSet.title: Book Notices
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

INSIGHT THERAPY. By Tibor Agoston. Ohio: Department of Mental Hygiene & Correction, 1969, 334 pp.

In his attempt to cover the entire field of psychoanalysis, the author divides his book, obviously intended as a primer of therapy for beginning psychiatrists, into five divisions of unequal logical levels—historical background, psychosystematics, applied methodology, differential dynamics, and sexual deviations. Because of the enormous amount of material covered (reflected in the 203 items listed by page in the table of contents), each subject discussed—ranging from the topographic point of view through practically every individual aspect of theory, symptomatology, and technique—is presented in very brief fashion.

BASIC PSYCHOANALYTIC CONCEPTS ON THE LIBIDO THEORY. VOLUME I. Edited by Humberto Nagera et al. New York: Basic Books, 1969, 194 pp., $6.50.

This is the first of a series of four volumes (two currently published) resulting from a six-year study by a group of 15 analysts and child psychotherapists from The Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic (the Director of which is Anna Freud). Carried on under the chairmanship of Dr. Nagera, this project's purpose is to assist in the understanding of psychoanalytic thought and terminology, to clarify concepts and trace the history of their evolution, to further the aim of reducing the confusion in current psychoanalytic literature by providing authors with more precise definitions of terms and concepts, and to facilitate the efforts of students of psychoanalysis to pursue independent courses of study with a critical and constructive approach to theory formation. This volume traces the development of Freud's ideas on the libido theory, the subject matter ranging from the "Source, Pressure, Aim and Object of the Sexual Component Instinct" to "The Castration Complex." An unusually detailed bibliography is provided.

BASIC PSYCHOANALYTIC CONCEPTS ON THE THEORY OF DREAMS. VOLUME II. Edited by Humberto Nagera et al. New York: Basic Books, 1969, 128 pp., $5.00.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Alfred J. Siegman, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>240 East 79 Street</ln><ln>New York, N.Y. 10021</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: A Note on the Complexity Surrounding a Temporary Use of Denial
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Note on the Complexity Surrounding a Temporary Use of Denial

Alfred Siegman M.D.

Alfred Siegman

Alfred J. Siegman, M.D.
240 East 79 Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Alfred Siegman  

IT IS GENERALLY accepted that the absence of sustained grief and mourning in very young children1 and the subsequent amnesia in later life are primarily the result of the denial and secondary repression of the object loss. The choice and continuation of this order of global, archaic defense is only possible when there is either marked immaturity or gross defectiveness of the ego with a concomitant lack of attainment or impairment of reality testing. Yet the denial of object loss and especially the absence, or attenuation, of associated affects of grief and mourning are also frequently encountered after childhood. However, in these instances a denial and total repression cannot be effected in the crude and direct fashion described above.

In later life, where marked pathology of the ego exists and reality testing is also proportionately defective, denial can often be maintained solely by means of a split in the ego in a manner that is dynamically analogous to the process observed in the perversions.2

Submitted November 1, 1968

1The controversy surrounding the views of Bowlby (1960) in regard to grief and mourning are beyond the scope of this paper.2As in the perversions, derivatives of the denied and unconscious perception may appear in symptoms or in acting out.372

responseSet.title: Snout-Hand Behavior in an Adult Patient
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Snout-Hand Behavior in an Adult Patient

Carl Adatto M.D.

Carl Adatto

Carl P. Adatto, M.D.
6205 Garfield Street
New Orleans, Louisiana 70118

Carl Adatto  

SUMMARY

This paper, through use of clinical excerpts, demonstrates and discusses the origins and meanings of snout-hand behavior as an example of nonverbal communication in the psychoanalysis of an adult male patient. Knowledge of the origins of such behavior in the fetal and infantile periods is useful in making a connection with data yielded in an adult analysis. The variety of meanings of nonverbal activity points to the complexity of nonverbal behavior observed in the analytic situation. The analytic method aims to change the nonverbal behavior to verbal, from repeating to remembering, and from acting out to analysis of the conflict rooted in the infantile period. Therefore, nonverbal activities of a patient are important areas for analysis in that they often contain significant neurotic meaning.

responseSet.title: Social Deprivation in Childhood and Character Formation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Social Deprivation in Childhood and Character Formation

Jeanne Spurlock M.D.

Jeanne Spurlock

Jeanne Spurlock, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Meharry Medical College
Nashville, Tenn. 37208

Jeanne Spurlock  

Reginald Lourie opened the panel by emphasizing the relevancy of psychoanalytic data to many current social problems, saying that "psychoanalytic information has provided the core basis on which many of the human problems involved can be understood and therefore approached." Child psychoanalysts especially have been involved in utilizing psychoanalytic information to assist in resolving root problems of communities. Lourie reminded the audience that deprivation as an influence on child development can occur at any level in society, but the focus of the panel was on those types of rearing imposed by poverty with its fostering of dependency, hopelessness, helplessness, and passivity.

Humberto Nagera's presentation concerned "deprivation in the earlier stages of life, when lack of appropriate stimulation (or lack of protection from excessive stimuli) may distort or stunt the growth of brain structures, or, in psychoanalytic terms, distort the growth of the somatic and psychological ego apparatuses." He referred to the somatic ego apparatus underlying any given ego function and the qualitative differences—"innate givens"—in these organic structures from one individual to another. Recent data suggests that much that has been considered innate can be shown to have been acquired during the first year of life. The genetic potential, in terms of the development of the anatomical structures of the brain, must continue after birth, if it is to reach its fulfillment. This continuation is dependent on internal, embryological maturational forces and the interaction of such forces with varying forms of external stimulation.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1969. Chairman: Reginald Lourie, M.D.

622

responseSet.title: Factors in the Psychoanalytic Treatment of Narcissistic Personalities
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Factors in the Psychoanalytic Treatment of Narcissistic Personalities

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
The Menninger Foundation
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Otto Kernberg  

SUMMARY

A general hypothesis regarding the etiology of the narcissistic personality structure is proposed, involving the relationships between pathological narcissism and pathological object relationships. Technical problems in the psychoanalytic treatment of narcissistic personalities are examined—especially their typical transference resistances—and prognostic criteria are outlined.

responseSet.documentRef: Gedo, J. E. (1970) Thoughts on Art in the Age of Freud—A Review. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:219-245

responseSet.title: Thoughts on Art in the Age of Freud—A Review
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on Art in the Age of Freud—A Review

John Gedo M.D.

John Gedo

John E. Gedo, M.D.
30 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill, 60602

John Gedo  

The eminent English critic, Sir Herbert Read, values an aesthetic object to the degree that it serves to express a pattern of emotion symbolically. He has written (1951) that aesthetics must consequently be based in part on a depth psychology capable of elucidating hidden symbolism, i.e., of showing the abstract, nonrepresentational nature of symbols. Psychoanalysis has therefore served as an indispensable tool in the development of art

From the Department of Psychiatry, University of Illinois, College of Medicine, Chicago.

The following recent books on the visual arts from the viewpoint of depth psychology will be considered:

American Imago: GENIUS, PSYCHOPATHOLOGY AND CREATIVITY. Spring-Summer, 1967 (Vol. 24, Nos. 1 & 2).

Calas, Nicholas: ART IN THE AGE OF RISK. New York: E. P. Dutton, 1968.

Ehrenzweig, Anton: THE HIDDEN ORDER OF ART. Los Angeles & Berkeley: University of California Press, 1967.

Havelka, Jaroslav: THE NATURE OF THE CREATIVE PROCESS IN ART. The Hague: Martinus Nijhoff, 1968.

Nagera, Humberto: VINCENT VAN GOGH. New York: International Universities Press. 1967.

Slochower, Harry, Editor: PSYCHOANALYSIS AS AN ART AND A SCIENCE. Detroit: Wayne State University Press, 1967.

Stokes, Adrian: PAINTING AND THE INNER WORLD. London: Tavistock Publications, 1963.

Waelder, Robert: PSYCHOANALYTIC AVENUES TO ART. New York: International Universities Press, 1965.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>Menninger Foundation</ln><ln>Topeka, kansas 66601</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: A Psychoanalytic Classification of Character Pathology
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Classification of Character Pathology

Otto Kernberg M.D.

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg, M.D.
Menninger Foundation
Topeka, kansas 66601

Otto Kernberg  

SUMMARY

In this paper I propose a classification of character pathology in the attempt (1) to establish psychoanalytic criteria for differential diagnoses among different types and degrees of severity of character

819

pathology; (2) to clarify the relationship between a descriptive characterological diagnosis and a metapsychological, especially structural, analysis; and (3) to arrange subgroups of character pathology according to their degree of severity.

This classification reflects a conviction as to the usefulness of a diagnostic study of patients involving structural and geneticdynamic considerations in addition to purely descriptive ones. The developments in psychoanalytic technique and in modified, psychoanalytically oriented procedures have provided us with a broad armamentarium of psychotherapeutic tools. The development of diagnostic criteria derived from psychoanalytic theory could improve our capacity for optimal individualization of psychological treatment.

responseSet.documentRef: Pulver, S. E. (1970) Narcissism—The Term and the Concept. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:319-341
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Pulver, S. E. (1970) Narcissism—The Term and the Concept. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:319-341
responseSet.documentRefXML: Pulver, S. E. (1970) Narcissism—The Term and the Concept. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:319-341
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 197018319-341Narcissism—The Term and the ConceptSydneyE.Pulver Sydney E. Pulver, M.D.Pennsylvania Hospital111 North 49 StreetPhiladelphia, Pa. 19139
responseSet.title: Narcissism—The Term and the Concept
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism—The Term and the Concept

Sydney Pulver M.D.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney E. Pulver, M.D.
Pennsylvania Hospital
111 North 49 Street
Philadelphia, Pa. 19139

Sydney Pulver  

SUMMARY

In common with many of the other human sciences, psychoanalysis is constantly trying to redefine its terms to gain greater specificity and clarity of meaning. This paper looks at one of our most important concepts, narcissism, and the various applications of the term in our literature.

The term narcissism was originally used to name a number of phenomena which appeared to be explainable by assuming that the individual had taken himself as a sexual object—that he loved himself or some part of himself. Gradually, the application of the term was extended to almost any concentration of psychological interest upon the self. Many different phenomena fell into that category, and narcissism was applied to all of them. Very early in the psychoanalytic literature the term was being used in several different ways:

1. Clinically, to denote a sexual perversion.

2. Genetically, to denote a stage of development.

3. In terms of object relationships, to denote two different phenomena:

a. a type of object choice,

b. a mode of relating to the environment.

4. To denote various aspects of the complex ego state of self-esteem.

The use of the term narcissism to describe so many different aspects of self-interests and so many different levels of abstraction has led to considerable confusion in the psychoanalytic literature. It is not uncommon to find two authors differing about narcissism on apparently theoretical grounds. On closer inspection, however, the difference is semantic rather than theoretical. Each of the authors is discussing different phenomena, both of which have been called narcissistic. A more serious result of this vagueness is that the concept has not received the elaboration in terms of ego psychology which has been given to many other psychoanalytic concepts, and which it richly deserves.

responseSet.documentRef: Olinick, S. L. (1970) Negative Therapeutic Reaction. Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association 18:655-672

<artinfo arttype="REP" artsource="A1V2" j="APA" artsubtype="REP" id="APA.018.0655A" ISSN="0003-0651"><artyear>1970</artyear><artvol actual="18">18</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">655-672</artpgrg><arttitle>Negative Therapeutic Reaction</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Olinick, Stanley L." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Stanley</nfirst><nmid>L.</nmid><nlast>Olinick</nlast><ndeg dgr="OTHER" other="M.D."/></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Stanley L. Olinick, M.D.</cs></ln><ln>4545 Connecticut Avenue, N.W., Suite 306</ln><ln>Washington, D.C. 20008</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Negative Therapeutic Reaction
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Negative Therapeutic Reaction

Stanley Olinick M.D.

Stanley Olinick

Stanley L. Olinick, M.D.
4545 Connecticut Avenue, N.W., Suite 306
Washington, D.C. 20008

Stanley Olinick  

That the topic of negative therapeutic reaction was a well-chosen one was made evident by what newspapers like to call "a capacity audience attendance." The proliferating nouns indicate that this topic, more than half a century in age, continues to be of great interest and essential to the discriminating understanding of a large group of clinical and theoretical problems. That it is not a thoroughly resolved problem was demonstrated by the explicit disagreements among the panelists; but it was clear that it is resolvable, and that there is a large area of ready agreement about the syndrome's phenomenology and metapsychology.

Milton H. Horowitz opened with a thorough and comprehensive review, placing the concept of the negative therapeutic reaction in perspective. Regrettably, his presentation can be given here only in condensed version.

Freud referred to the worsening of symptoms during psychoanalytic treatment in his technical papers of 1913-1917. He first referred to "negative reactions" in the case history of the Wolf Man. There he commented on the patient's habit of producing transitory "negative reactions" when something had been conclusively cleared up; aggravation of the symptoms contradicted the effects of the analytic work. He reflected that children often respond to prohibitions in this way, repeating the act once more after the interdiction, as though to gain the point both of stopping of their own accord and of disobeying the prohibition. By 1918, Freud viewed exacerbation of symptoms, following their analysis, as instances of negativistic defiance related to the anal-sadistic phase of libidinal development.

His views underwent a considerable alteration by the time he wrote The Ego and the Id. The negative therapeutic reaction occurred in "certain people" who, when one spoke hopefully to them, or expressed satisfaction with the course of the treatment, or interpreted accurately, showed an exacerbation of their condition.
